questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Transfer subject to mortgage - no personal liability of grantee
questions.stem: A homeowner borrowed $300,000 from a bank and gave the bank a note secured by a mortgage on her house. The homeowner later sold the house to a buyer. The deed stated that the buyer took the property “subject to” the existing mortgage, but the buyer did not assume the mortgage note and made no promise to pay the debt. The homeowner later defaulted, and the bank foreclosed. The foreclosure sale produced less than the amount owed. The bank sued the buyer personally for the deficiency.
questions.call: Is the buyer personally liable for the deficiency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because anyone who buys mortgaged land automatically becomes personally liable on the note.
choices.rationale: Personal liability requires assumption or another promise; taking subject to a mortgage alone does not create it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the bank may always recover a deficiency from the current owner of the land.
choices.rationale: The mortgage lien may be enforced against the land, but personal deficiency liability depends on the buyer's promise to pay.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the buyer took subject to the mortgage but did not assume personal liability for the debt.
choices.rationale: The buyer risks losing the property to foreclosure but is not personally liable for a deficiency absent assumption.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a foreclosure sale always extinguishes the underlying note in full.
choices.rationale: Foreclosure does not necessarily eliminate the debt as to personally liable parties; the buyer simply is not one of them.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a grantee who takes land subject to a mortgage is personally liable for a deficiency.
explanation.governingRule: A grantee who assumes a mortgage becomes personally liable on the debt. A grantee who merely takes subject to the mortgage does not become personally liable, although the mortgage remains enforceable against the land.
explanation.application: The deed used subject-to language and the buyer made no promise to pay. The bank could foreclose the house but cannot recover a deficiency from the buyer personally.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating ownership of the mortgaged land as the same thing as liability on the mortgage note.
explanation.memoryAid: Subject to means the land is liable; assumption means the buyer is liable.

authoring.contentWarning: Original MBE-style practice content generated for study. Not official, released, licensed, or NCBE-authored content. A qualified attorney should verify current law, taxonomy, wording, and one-best-answer validity before publication.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Landlord-tenant - assignment of entire remaining lease term
questions.stem: A tenant leased a storefront from a landlord for five years at $4,000 per month. After one year, the tenant transferred all of her remaining leasehold interest to a retailer. The transfer document stated that the retailer would take over the premises for the entire remaining four years and pay rent directly to the landlord. The landlord did not release the original tenant. The retailer occupied the storefront for six months and then stopped paying rent.
questions.call: May the landlord recover unpaid rent from the retailer for the period of the retailer's possession?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the landlord did not sign the transfer document.
choices.rationale: An assignment can create privity of estate between landlord and assignee even without the landlord signing the assignment, absent a valid restriction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because transferring the entire remaining lease term made the retailer an assignee in privity of estate with the landlord.
choices.rationale: An assignee of the leasehold is liable on covenants that run with the land, including rent, while in privity of estate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the original tenant remains liable on the lease.
choices.rationale: The original tenant may remain liable in privity of contract, but that does not prevent the assignee from also being liable while in possession.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only because the transfer document used the word assignment.
choices.rationale: The legal effect turns on transfer of the entire remaining term, not merely on labels.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a transfer of the entire remaining lease term creates an assignment and assignee rent liability.
explanation.governingRule: A transfer of the tenant's entire remaining leasehold interest is an assignment. The assignee is in privity of estate with the landlord and is liable for covenants running with the land, such as rent, while the assignee holds the leasehold.
explanation.application: The retailer received the entire remaining four-year term and occupied the premises. The landlord may recover rent from the retailer for the period of privity of estate.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming the original tenant's continuing liability eliminates the assignee's liability.
explanation.memoryAid: Whole remaining term means assignment; assignment brings rent liability while held.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: License by estoppel - reliance on permission to use land
questions.stem: A landowner orally permitted a neighbor to build and use a gravel driveway across the landowner's property to reach the neighbor's garage. The landowner watched without objection while the neighbor spent $25,000 constructing the driveway in the exact location discussed. After the driveway was completed and used for several months, the landowner revoked permission and blocked the driveway. The neighbor sought an order allowing continued use.
questions.call: Is the neighbor likely to obtain relief?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because an oral permission to use land is always revocable at any time, regardless of reliance.
choices.rationale: A license ordinarily is revocable, but estoppel may make it irrevocable when the licensee reasonably and substantially relies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because an easement can arise only by a signed deed.
choices.rationale: Although express easements generally require writing, reliance-based doctrines may protect use despite the absence of a deed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the neighbor reasonably relied on the landowner's permission by making substantial improvements.
choices.rationale: A license may become irrevocable by estoppel to the extent necessary to avoid injustice after substantial reliance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the neighbor used the driveway adversely for the full prescriptive period before revocation.
choices.rationale: The claim is based on reliance on permission, not adverse use for prescription.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether reliance can make an oral license to use land irrevocable.
explanation.governingRule: A license to use land is ordinarily revocable, but a licensor may be estopped from revoking when the licensee reasonably and substantially changes position in reliance on the permission and revocation would be unjust.
explanation.application: The landowner expressly permitted the driveway and watched the neighbor spend substantial money building it. Blocking the driveway after that reliance supports relief.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating every oral land-use permission as freely revocable even after induced expenditures.
explanation.memoryAid: Permission plus substantial reliance can harden a license into an irrevocable right.

authorityNotes: Common-law easement by estoppel / irrevocable license doctrine
